title: Visual-Mode Tricks
subtitle: Block {key:O} for diagonal corners. {key:gv} to re-select.
summary: Two tricks that make *visual mode* much more useful: {key:O} in block mode swaps to the opposite diagonal corner, and {key:g}{key:v} re-selects the previous selection.

blocks.text: Two small tricks save lots of pain in *visual mode*: {key:O} (capital O) for moving across the diagonal of a block, and {key:g}{key:v} for getting back the selection you just lost.

In character-wise or linewise *visual mode,* {key:o} swaps which end of the selection your motions move. In *visual-block mode,* {key:o} swaps along one diagonal — but {key:O} swaps to the *opposite* corner of the rectangle. So you can extend the block from any of the four corners without restarting.

blocks.label: (in *visual-block mode*) Move cursor to opposite corner of rectangle

blocks.text: Pressed {key:Esc} by accident? Did one operation and want to do another on the same range? {key:g}{key:v} restores the last visual selection — same anchor, same cursor, same mode.

blocks.title: How {key:g}{key:v} Works
blocks.text:
Whenever you leave *visual mode*, Vim writes two marks ({key:`}{key:<}, {key:`}{key:>}) and remembers the *type* of the selection. {key:g}{key:v} re-enters that type of Visual with the cursor and anchor restored from those marks.

Because it's just two marks, anything that updates them updates {key:g}{key:v}'s target. `:s` commands run on the visual range update them implicitly — so you can `:'<,'>s/foo/bar/g` and then {key:g}{key:v} to grab the same range again.

The range works *per buffer*. Open a different buffer; come back; {key:g}{key:v} still works.

rows: Move to opposite corner (*visual-block mode* only)

blocks.title: {key:o} swaps the anchor, not the text
blocks.text: In *visual mode*, {key:o} (and {key:O} for visual-block) flips which endpoint is the anchor and which is the cursor. The selection contents don't change — only which end your motions extend. After an operator runs, the cursor lands on whichever endpoint was current at consume time, so swapping ends before {key:d} or {key:y} changes where you end up.
